title: Primitive Data Types: Float and Double

content.problemStatement:
In C, both `float` and `double` are used to store **floating-point numbers**, which are numbers with decimal points.

* `float`: A single-precision data type that occupies **4** bytes of memory and can hold up to 7 decimal digits.
* `double`: A double-precision data type that occupies **8** bytes of memory and can hold up to 15 decimal digits. A `double` has a higher range than a `float` in C, as it is a 64-bit floating-point number.

**Syntax:**

```c
float variable_name = value;  // Declaration and assignment of a float variable
double variable_name = value; // Declaration and assignment of a double variable
```

---

**Example:**

```c
#include

int main() {
    // Declaring a float variable
    float floatVar = 3.123456789f; // The f suffix indicates that this value should be treated as a float.

    // Declaring a double variable
    double doubleVar = 3.123456789123456; // Value assigned to a double variable with higher decimal places

    // Printing the values of float and double with full precision
    // Format specifier %f for float and %lf for double. However, %f works for both float and double in C
    printf("Value of floatVar (float): %f\n", floatVar); // Printing float value
    printf("Value of doubleVar (double): %lf\n", doubleVar); // Printing double value

    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```default
Value of floatVar (float): 3.123457
Value of doubleVar (double): 3.123457
```

---

**Note:**

In C, when using `%f` or `%lf` in `printf`, the default precision is **6** decimal places, even though `double` has more precision internally. This means that even if `doubleVar` holds more precision (15–16 digits), `printf` will display only the first **6** decimal places by default — unless specified otherwise (which will be covered in upcoming modules).
